_meta.description: Functional design patterns for FDM printing. Each pattern describes a common mechanical feature with material requirements, dimensional constraints, and print orientation rules.

snap_fit_cantilever.description: A flexible arm with a hook that deflects during assembly and snaps into a mating feature. The most common snap fit type.
snap_fit_cantilever.print_orientation_reason: Layer lines along the arm length create a failure plane at the base. Print so the arm flexes parallel to the build plate.
agent_guidance: PLA snap fits break after 2-3 cycles. ALWAYS recommend PETG or Nylon for repeated assembly/disassembly.
Add a fillet (0.5mm min radius) at the arm base — stress concentration causes 80% of snap fit failures.
For tool-free assembly, keep insertion force under 5N. This means limiting deflection to 1-2% of arm length.
Print with 100% infill in the arm region if the slicer supports variable infill. Otherwise, 40% minimum.
Test fit tolerance on your specific printer — 0.3mm is typical but can vary by 0.1mm between machines.

press_fit.description: A joint where one part is slightly larger than the hole it fits into, creating a friction-based connection. No fasteners needed.
press_fit.print_orientation_reason: Holes printed vertically are rounder and more dimensionally accurate than holes printed horizontally (which become slightly oval due to layer stacking).
agent_guidance: Print a test piece first. Press fit tolerance is the most printer-dependent dimension in FDM.
Start with 0.1mm interference and adjust. Too tight = cracked part. Too loose = falls out.
Chamfer the entry (45 degrees, 0.5mm deep) to guide alignment and prevent edge chipping during assembly.
For bearing seats: make the hole 0.05mm smaller than the bearing OD. For magnets: 0.1mm smaller than magnet diameter.
Print hole axis vertically for best roundness. Horizontal holes sag at the top — overhang creates an oval.

living_hinge.description: A thin flexible section connecting two rigid sections, allowing repeated bending at a defined axis. Like the hinge on a shampoo bottle cap.
living_hinge.print_orientation_reason: Layer adhesion is the weak point. Hinge must flex along layer lines, not across them, or it snaps on first bend.
agent_guidance: ONLY use TPU or Nylon. All other common FDM materials will snap within 1-10 cycles.
Nylon: 0.4-0.6mm thickness, thousands of cycles. TPU: 0.3-0.8mm thickness, virtually unlimited cycles.
Print orientation is CRITICAL — get it wrong and the hinge snaps on first bend regardless of material.
Add a radius at the fold (1mm minimum). Sharp folds concentrate stress and cut hinge life by 10x.
Test with a prototype — hinge behavior is highly sensitive to exact thickness and material batch.

threaded_connection.description: Screw threads printed directly into the part. Can be external (bolt) or internal (nut). Works for both mating printed parts and accepting standard fasteners.
threaded_connection.print_orientation_reason: Vertical thread axis produces the most consistent thread profile. Horizontal threads have flat spots on the top from overhangs.
agent_guidance: Use coarse threads (2mm+ pitch). Fine threads (< 1.5mm) are unresolvable on most FDM printers at 0.2mm layer height.
Add 0.2mm clearance between mating threads. They won't screw together without it.
For standard fastener threads (M3, M4, etc.), print the hole undersized and tap with a real tap. Or use heat-set inserts.
Heat-set brass inserts are ALWAYS better than printed internal threads for metal screws. Recommend them for any load-bearing threaded connection.
Chamfer both ends of the thread to ease starting. Printed threads are fragile at the entry — chamfer prevents cross-threading.

cantilever_bracket.description: A rigid bracket that supports weight while mounted to a wall or surface. Must resist bending and shear loads.
cantilever_bracket.print_orientation_reason: Strongest orientation places layer lines parallel to the wall surface. The load then compresses across layers rather than pulling them apart.
agent_guidance: NEVER use PLA for load-bearing brackets. PLA creeps under sustained load and will slowly deform over weeks/months.
Add a triangular gusset (45 degrees) for any shelf arm longer than 80mm. Without it, the bracket relies entirely on the wall joint — which fails.
3mm fillet at the bracket-to-wall joint. Sharp corners concentrate stress and are where brackets snap.
Use 2 mounting screws MINIMUM, spaced vertically. Single-screw mounts rotate under load.
Print flat with the wall-mount surface on the bed. 100% infill for brackets under 50g. 60% minimum for larger ones.
For loads over 5 kg, use 4mm+ wall thickness and consider printing in Nylon or Polycarbonate.
Rule of thumb for PETG: 4mm x 20mm cross-section supports ~5 kg at 100mm cantilever length. Scale linearly for different loads.

watertight_container.description: A container designed to hold liquid without leaking through the print walls or seams.
watertight_container.print_orientation_reason: Container opens upward so no supports are needed inside. The base is printed directly on the bed plate for a smooth, sealed bottom.
agent_guidance: PETG is the best watertight material — excellent layer adhesion means fewer micro-gaps between lines.
Use 4+ wall shells (perimeters). 2 walls WILL leak through the seam between inner and outer walls.
Vase mode (spiral outer contour) creates a single continuous wall with no seam — naturally watertight for simple shapes.
For vase mode: 0.6mm nozzle + 0.8mm line width, or 0.4mm nozzle + 0.5mm line width. The wider the line, the more watertight.
Nylon absorbs water — containers swell over time. Avoid for anything that stays wet.
For food contact (cups, bowls): PETG is food-safe. PLA is conditionally safe but micro-gaps harbor bacteria. Apply food-safe epoxy coating for PLA.
6+ bottom layers. The bottom is where hydrostatic pressure is highest — thin bottoms leak first.

enclosure_box.description: A multi-part box designed to house electronics, with provisions for cable routing, mounting, ventilation, and assembly.
enclosure_box.print_orientation_reason: Print box halves with the opening facing up. This eliminates supports inside the box and gives the best surface finish on the exterior.
agent_guidance: Design the lid with a lip (2mm deep, 0.3mm clearance). This self-aligns the lid and prevents dust/light ingress.
Add corner fillets (2mm radius) on all edges — sharp printed corners are weak and chip.
PCB standoffs: 3mm height, 2.5mm hole for M2.5 screws or self-tapping screws. Add a chamfer on top for screw alignment.
Ventilation: 2mm wide slots in rows. Calculate total open area based on component heat output. 1 cm2 per watt is a starting rule.
Cable entry: round holes with a small keyhole cutout so cables route in from the side, not forced through from above.
For outdoor electronics: use ASA material + add a drip edge above ventilation slots so water runs off.
Use snap-fit lid closure for tool-free access, or screw bosses for secure closure. Don't use both.

gear.description: A toothed wheel designed to mesh with another gear to transmit rotational motion and torque.
gear.print_orientation_reason: Teeth printed vertically (face on bed) have consistent profiles. Teeth printed sideways have layer stairstepping that creates rough meshing and accelerated wear.
agent_guidance: Nylon is the ideal gear material — self-lubricating, fatigue-resistant, and excellent wear properties.
Module 1.0 is the minimum for FDM at 0.2mm layer height. Below that, teeth don't resolve cleanly.
Module 1.5 (recommended) gives more forgiving tolerances and smoother meshing on typical printers.
Add 0.15mm backlash between mating gears. Without it, gears bind and strip teeth.
PLA gears work for display/toy use but wear rapidly under real torque. Don't recommend for functional gear trains.
For metal axle + printed gear: size the bore 0.1mm smaller than the shaft and press fit. Or use a D-shaft with matching D-bore.
Print with gear face flat on the bed, 100% infill, 0.12mm layer height for best tooth profile accuracy.
Lubricate nylon gear meshes with white lithium grease. Even self-lubricating nylon lasts 3-5x longer with grease.

heat_set_insert_boss.description: A cylindrical boss designed to accept M2-M5 brass heat-set threaded inserts, providing strong metal threads in a printed part for repeated screw assembly.
heat_set_insert_boss.print_orientation_reason: Vertical boss axis gives the best hole roundness and allows the soldering iron to press straight down, melting plastic evenly around the insert.
agent_guidance: Size the hole 0.1-0.2mm SMALLER than the insert outer diameter. The insert melts into the surrounding plastic — too large a hole and it pulls out.
Boss outer diameter should be 2x the insert OD. Thinner walls crack from thermal expansion during insertion.
Use 100% infill in the boss region. Low infill creates voids that the insert melts into unevenly, causing misalignment.
PLA works but has low heat deflection — the insert can loosen over time under load or in warm environments. ABS/ASA/Nylon are far more reliable.
Add a 45-degree chamfer at the hole entry to guide the insert and prevent the soldering iron tip from slipping off-center.

cable_management_clip.description: A snap-on clip that secures cables to surfaces or panels, using a flexible arm that opens to accept the cable and snaps closed to retain it.
cable_management_clip.print_orientation_reason: Printing with the clip opening facing up keeps the snap arms in the XY plane, where layer adhesion supports the flex cycle without delamination.
agent_guidance: The clip gap should be ~80% of cable diameter — wide enough to push the cable in, narrow enough to retain it under gravity and vibration.
Snap arm length of 8-12mm gives a good balance between insertion force and retention. Shorter arms require more force and fatigue faster.
PETG is the best general-purpose clip material — flexible enough for snap cycles, stiff enough for retention, and doesn't creep like PLA.
For vibration environments (printers, vehicles), use Nylon or TPU clips. Rigid materials like PLA crack from cyclic vibration fatigue.
Add a small taper (10%) on the snap arm tip to create a ramp that guides the cable in. Blunt tips require too much force and feel cheap.

wall_mount_bracket.description: A flat or L-shaped bracket for mounting objects to walls or surfaces using screws, with provisions for screw clearance and load distribution.
wall_mount_bracket.print_orientation_reason: Printing the mounting face flat on the bed maximizes cross-sectional strength across layers and gives a smooth surface for flush wall contact.
agent_guidance: 4.2mm holes for #8 wood screws, 5.5mm for 1/4-inch lag bolts. Always oversize holes by 0.2mm — printed holes shrink slightly.
Minimum 4mm of material around each screw hole. Less than that and the bracket cracks when you tighten the screw.
Counterbore screw heads 2.5mm deep so they sit flush. Protruding screw heads create a gap between the bracket and the mounted object.
For L-brackets, add a fillet or gusset at the bend — sharp 90-degree printed corners are the weakest point and fail under sustained load.
PLA brackets creep under constant load (gravity pulling on a shelf). PETG or ASA are much better for anything that holds weight 24/7.

phone_stand.description: An angled stand that holds a phone or tablet at a comfortable viewing angle, with a front lip for retention and a stable base to prevent tipping.
phone_stand.print_orientation_reason: Printing with the back panel flat on the bed avoids supports for the angled face and produces a smooth rear surface for desk contact.
agent_guidance: Base depth must be at least 60mm to prevent tipping with a phone. For tablets (heavier), increase to 80-100mm or add a weighted base.
The front lip needs to be 15mm minimum — enough to catch the phone but not cover the bottom of the screen. Add a charging cable slot in the lip.
60 degrees is the sweet spot viewing angle for desk use. 30 degrees is too flat (hard to see), 75 degrees is too upright (tips over easily).
Add 0.5mm deep horizontal grooves on the base and inside the slot — these prevent the phone from sliding on smooth surfaces.
PLA is fine for stands — they don't flex, don't bear sustained load, and benefit from PLA's rigidity. Silk PLA makes attractive desk accessories.

ball_joint_socket.description: A spherical joint allowing multi-axis rotation, consisting of a ball on one part and a receiving socket on another. Can be print-in-place or assembled.
ball_joint_socket.print_orientation_reason: Printing with the socket opening upward allows the ball interior to bridge cleanly and avoids supports inside the socket that would fuse the joint.
agent_guidance: 0.4-0.6mm clearance between ball and socket is the sweet spot for FDM. Less than 0.4mm fuses together; more than 0.6mm is sloppy.
Socket opening should be 65-75% of ball diameter — large enough to snap the ball in, small enough to retain it under load.
For print-in-place joints, use a 0.3mm bridge layer between ball and socket. Break it free after printing by rotating the joint.
Nylon gives the smoothest joint motion due to its self-lubricating properties. PETG is the next best choice for smooth rotation.
Avoid carbon fiber filled materials — the fibers are abrasive and will wear the socket out quickly, creating a loose sloppy joint.

dovetail_joint.description: A trapezoidal tongue-and-groove joint that slides together for tool-free mechanical assembly. Resists pull-apart forces perpendicular to the slide direction.
dovetail_joint.print_orientation_reason: The slide axis should be horizontal (parallel to bed) so the dovetail cross-section is printed accurately in the XY plane, avoiding stairstepping on the tapered faces.
agent_guidance: 10-15 degree taper angle is optimal. Less than 10 degrees and the joint pulls apart too easily; more than 15 degrees and it jams.
Add 0.2-0.3mm clearance on each side of the dovetail. Test fit on your printer — dovetails are very sensitive to dimensional accuracy.
PLA and CF-PLA are excellent because rigidity makes the slide feel precise. Flexible materials like Nylon feel sloppy in dovetails.
Minimum 20mm slide length for a secure joint. Shorter dovetails can rock and wiggle under lateral loads.
Add a 0.5mm chamfer at the entry of both halves to guide alignment. Without it, the pieces catch on the first layer elephant's foot.

battery_compartment.description: A holder designed to securely house standard battery cells with contact terminals, polarity guides, and a retaining lid for easy replacement.
battery_compartment.print_orientation_reason: Printing with the battery opening facing up creates the cell cavity without supports and gives a smooth bed-contact surface for the compartment floor.
agent_guidance: Add 0.5mm clearance around the battery cell. Batteries need to drop in and out easily — too tight and they get stuck, especially with wrapper swell.
Recess the spring contact terminals 2mm into the wall so they compress when the battery is inserted. Without a recess, springs push the battery out.
For 18650 cells: inner dimensions 18.5mm x 65.5mm. For AA: 14.8mm x 51mm. For CR2032: 20.5mm diameter, 3.5mm depth.
Add embossed or engraved polarity markings (+ and -) at 0.4mm depth. Users will insert batteries wrong without clear markings.
Slide lids are more reliable than snap lids for battery compartments — snap lids weaken over repeated open/close cycles and eventually stop latching.

pcb_enclosure_standoff.description: Cylindrical posts that elevate a PCB above the enclosure floor, providing clearance for bottom-side components and secure mounting via screws or snap-fit nubs.
pcb_enclosure_standoff.print_orientation_reason: Printing standoffs vertically produces round, accurate holes and avoids the need for supports inside the screw holes.
agent_guidance: Standard PCB mounting holes are M2.5 (2.7mm clearance) or M3 (3.2mm clearance). Check the board datasheet before designing.
Outer diameter should be at least 2x the hole diameter. M3 hole needs a 7mm OD standoff minimum to avoid splitting when tightening screws.
Add a 0.5mm fillet at the standoff base where it meets the enclosure floor. Standoffs without fillets snap off under PCB insertion force.
For tool-free PCB mounting, add a 0.5mm snap-fit nub at the top of each standoff instead of a screw hole. The PCB clicks into place.
3mm standoff height clears most through-hole solder joints. Use 5mm if the PCB has bottom-side components like capacitors or connectors.

gopro_mount.description: A quick-release mounting interface compatible with the GoPro two/three-prong system, allowing attachment to the extensive ecosystem of GoPro accessories.
gopro_mount.print_orientation_reason: Printing with fingers oriented vertically produces the most accurate finger spacing and width, which is critical for mating with standard GoPro accessories.
agent_guidance: GoPro standard: 2-prong (female) mates with 3-prong (male). Finger width is 3.0mm, gap between fingers is 3.5mm. These dimensions are not negotiable.
The mounting hole is 5.5mm for the standard GoPro thumb screw (M5). Undersize this hole and the screw won't fit; oversize and the mount wobbles.
Print in PETG, ASA, or CF-PLA for vibration environments (bikes, vehicles). PLA mounts crack from cyclic vibration fatigue within weeks.
Add 0.5mm fillets at the base of each finger. Sharp corners concentrate stress where the fingers meet the base plate — the most common failure point.
Test fit with a real GoPro accessory before final print. Finger spacing tolerance of +/- 0.15mm is the difference between a snug fit and a useless mount.

pegboard_hook.description: Hooks and holders designed to insert into standard 25.4mm (1-inch) spaced pegboard holes for tool and accessory organization.
pegboard_hook.print_orientation_reason: Printing with the peg axis vertical produces round, accurate pegs that slide smoothly into pegboard holes. Horizontal pegs are oval from layer stacking.
agent_guidance: Standard pegboard holes are 6.35mm (1/4 inch). Print pegs at 4.0mm diameter with 0.2mm clearance — they need to slide in easily but not rattle.
Add a 1mm lip at the back of each peg insertion. This prevents the hook from pulling forward out of the pegboard under load.
PETG hooks hold ~5 kg safely. PLA hooks hold ~2 kg but creep under sustained load. For heavy tools, use PETG or CF-PLA.
Use 2 pegs minimum (25.4mm spacing) for any hook that will hold more than 1 kg. Single-peg hooks rotate and pull out under load.
Print at 100% infill — pegboard hooks are small and the pegs need maximum strength. Sparse infill creates weak spots right where the load concentrates.

hinge_pin.description: A rotation joint using alternating knuckles held together by a cylindrical pin, allowing controlled rotation for doors, lids, and flip mechanisms.
hinge_pin.print_orientation_reason: Printing with the hinge axis horizontal (parallel to the bed) puts layer lines along the knuckle length, making them strongest against the rotational shear forces.
agent_guidance: Use an odd number of knuckles (3 or 5) so the two hinge halves have unequal counts. This centers the pin load and prevents the halves from shifting laterally.
0.3mm clearance between pin and knuckle bore. Tighter than 0.2mm and the pin won't insert; looser than 0.5mm and the hinge wobbles.
For removable pins, add a 0.5mm chamfer at each knuckle entry and use a pin with a cap or cotter pin hole for retention.
Knuckle wall thickness of 2mm minimum. Thinner knuckles split when you press the pin in, especially in PLA.
For 270-degree rotation (e.g., a lid that folds all the way back), offset the hinge axis so the knuckles clear the body at full extension.

latch_clasp.description: A lever-actuated latch mechanism for securing lids, doors, and panels on enclosures. Uses a hook-and-catch system with a spring arm to maintain engagement.
latch_clasp.print_orientation_reason: Printing the lever flat on the bed aligns layer lines along the lever length, maximizing bending strength in the spring arm and pivot area.
agent_guidance: The spring arm (1.0mm thick) provides the latch tension. Thicker = harder to open but more secure. Thinner = easier but may not hold under vibration.
Hook depth of 1.5-2.0mm provides secure engagement. Deeper hooks are harder to release; shallower hooks pop open from vibration or bumps.
PETG and Nylon are the best latch materials — they flex without fatiguing. PLA spring arms snap after 50-100 cycles.
Add a 0.5mm fillet on the catch hook entry face. This creates a ramp that guides the hook in during closure and reduces the force needed to latch.
Lever length of 20mm is a good default — long enough for comfortable finger operation, short enough to not snag on things or break from side loads.
